title_en: The Third Generation: The Imams of Law and Hadith
content_en:
The third generation in the Siyar A'lam al-Nubala — the Atba' al-Tabi'een, or Followers of the Followers — encompasses the period roughly from the early 8th through the mid-9th century CE. This was the era in which the four great schools of Sunni law crystallized around their founding imams, in which the major hadith collections were compiled and systematized, and in which the great theological debates that would define Islamic orthodoxy were fought out with decisive results. Adh-Dhahabi's treatment of this generation is among the most detailed and the most intellectually engaged in the entire Siyar, because the figures it covers are those who established the frameworks within which Islamic learning has operated ever since.

The four Imams of the schools of law receive treatment in the Siyar that combines reverence with critical specificity. Imam Abu Hanifah al-Nu'man ibn Thabit (d. 150 AH) is acknowledged as the founder of the first systematically organized school of Islamic jurisprudence. His legal methodology — notable for its extensive use of analogical reasoning (qiyas) and juridical preference (istihsan) to address situations not directly covered by hadith — generated both enormous influence and significant criticism from those who felt it departed too far from the literal texts. Adh-Dhahabi's assessment of Abu Hanifah reflects the complexity of his legacy: he acknowledges his exceptional intelligence and the breadth of his legal achievement while noting that some hadith scholars of his era found weaknesses in specific aspects of his hadith transmission. This nuanced position — honoring the Imam while being honest about the scholarly controversies surrounding him — is characteristic of the Siyar's approach to the founders of the legal schools.

Imam Malik ibn Anas (d. 179 AH), the founder of the Maliki school and author of the Muwatta, receives one of the most admiring entries in this section of the Siyar. Adh-Dhahabi's treatment reflects the near-universal consensus among hadith scholars that Malik was among the most reliable and precise transmitters in the history of the tradition. Malik's famous selectivity — he accepted only a small number of teachers and was extremely cautious about transmitting from those whose reliability he doubted — made his own transmission an object of particular confidence. His legal methodology, anchored in the practice of the people of Medina (amal ahl al-Madinah) as a living embodiment of the prophetic tradition, is described by adh-Dhahabi with evident appreciation.

Imam Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i (d. 204 AH) had the additional distinction, beyond his legal achievement, of founding the formal discipline of usul al-fiqh — the principles of Islamic jurisprudence — with his Risalah, which established for the first time a systematic framework for how legal derivation from the sources should proceed. Adh-Dhahabi acknowledges both dimensions of his contribution: his extraordinary intellectual gifts, his mastery of Arabic language and literature alongside his command of hadith, and the transformation of Islamic legal theory that his Risalah inaugurated. The entry for al-Shafi'i in the Siyar draws on the substantial body of anecdotes about his learning and his interactions with other scholars of his era, painting a portrait of a man as remarkable for his personal qualities as for his intellectual achievements.

Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal (d. 241 AH) occupies a special place in the Siyar because of adh-Dhahabi's own theological orientation within the Hanbali-Athari tradition. The entry for Ahmad is among the longest and most detailed in the work, reflecting both his extraordinary importance as a hadith scholar — his Musnad is the largest single collection of hadith from a single compiler, containing over thirty thousand narrations — and his pivotal role in the Mihna, the inquisition ordered by the Abbasid caliph al-Ma'mun to compel scholars to affirm the Mutazilite doctrine of the created Quran. Ahmad's refusal to capitulate under torture, imprisonment, and intense pressure made him one of the most celebrated figures of resistance to state coercion in Islamic history. Adh-Dhahabi documents both the theological substance of the controversy and its personal dimension — the suffering Ahmad endured and his steadfast maintenance of what he believed to be the correct position — with evident admiration and emotional engagement.

Among the major hadith scholars of this generation, al-Bukhari (d. 256 AH) receives treatment commensurate with his towering status in the tradition. Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari was the compiler of the Sahih al-Bukhari, which the overwhelming scholarly consensus recognizes as the most rigorously authenticated hadith collection in existence. Adh-Dhahabi's entry for al-Bukhari covers his extraordinary memory — he reportedly memorized over a hundred thousand hadith with their chains, and six hundred thousand hadith in total — his travels in search of hadith, his strict criteria for inclusion in the Sahih, and the controversies he encountered in his later life when certain local scholars turned against him. The portrait is of a man of extraordinary technical gifts combined with personal piety and a principled commitment to the rigorous standards that made his Sahih what it became.

Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj (d. 261 AH), compiler of the other Sahih in the canonical pair (the Sahihayn), Abu Dawud (d. 275 AH), al-Tirmidhi (d. 279 AH), al-Nasa'i (d. 303 AH), and Ibn Majah (d. 273 AH) — the compilers of the remaining four of the six canonical hadith collections — all receive entries in the Siyar that adh-Dhahabi uses to document both the individual scholars and the context in which the great hadith compilation project of the 3rd century AH was accomplished. This section of the Siyar constitutes an indispensable guide to the generation that gave the Islamic world the textual foundations that would anchor its religious life for all subsequent centuries.
